rulings.Case Title: PETER MAKRAS ET AL VS. UCSF MEDICAL CENTER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Terminating Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON TUESDAY, FEBRUARY 5, 2019, LINE 1, Part 1 of 2 DEFENDANTS ERIC HANSEN, BRENDA STENGELE, and THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA'S Motion For Terminating Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Melinda Derish, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendants' motion is granted in part and denied in part as follows: The court declines to award terminating sanctions, which may only be awarded for discovery abuse "(t)o the extent authorized by the chapter governing any particular discovery method."(CCP 2023.030.) The chapter governing requests for admission does not authorize terminating sanctions. (See CCP section 2033.290) However, plaintiffs have disobeyed the court's August 8, 2018 order to serve Code compliant verified further responses to defendants' Requests for Admission, Set Two, Nos. 14-22 to Peter Makras and Requests for Admission, Set Two, Nos. 10-20 to Kari Makras. Therefore the matters involved in these requests for admission are hereby deemed admitted pursuant to CCP section 2033.290(e) and defendants' related motion. In regard to plaintiffs' disobeying the August 8, 2018 order to serve objection-free, complete and verified responses to defendants' Requests for Production of Documents, Set Four, the court declines to award terminating sanctions because due process requires that sanction awards go no further than is necessary to accomplish the purpose of discovery. (Newland v. Sup. Ct. (1995) 40 Cal.App.4th, 608, 613; Caryl Richards, Inc. v. Sup. Ct. (1961) 188 Cal.App.2d, 300, 305.) That purpose is accomplished here as to document request Nos. 46 - 54 and 61 by granting evidentiary sanctions to preclude plaintiffs from introducing any further documents into evidence that were not already produced by August 8, 2018 related to loss of income; payments by Medicare; payments by Medi-Cal; and plaintiffs' alleged contacts with defendants. As to document request Nos. 55-60 and 62, an issue preclusion sanction is granted to preclude plaintiffs from contesting that in a lawsuit they filed against the 3M Company plaintiffs claim Mr. Makras' leg injuries were caused by the Bair Hugger surgical warming device. Continues in part 2 of 2

rulings.Case Title: SCOTT BRUSCELLA VS. BELAMY MILLER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO DISMISS
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, February 5, 2019, 2018, Line 2, DEFENDANT BELAMY MILLER's MOTION TO DISMISS. Defendant Bellamy Miller's motion to dismiss the action for delay in prosecution pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure sections 583.410 and 583.420(a)(2)(B) and California Rules of Court 3.1340 and 3.1342 is granted. The complaint in this action was filed on October 24, 2016. Plaintiff failed to exercise reasonable diligence in the prosecution of this action. He did not serve defendant with the summons and complaint for over six months, causing the Court to issue an order to show cause. The delay was not attributable to settlement negotiations or discussions. Plaintiff was not reasonably diligent in either seeking or responding to discovery. Plaintiff failed timely to respond to proper discovery requests, resulting in an order granting Defendant's motion to compel responses to form interrogatories and awarding monetary sanctions against Plaintiff and his counsel. In the more than two years after the complaint was filed and before the instant motion was filed, plaintiff did not serve any discovery. (See Landry v. Berryessa Union School Dist. (1995) 39 Cal.App.4th 691, 699 [trial court properly dismissed action, where despite "plaintiffs' claims of diligent discovery efforts, they allowed seven and one-half months to go by without activity on the case" and allowed 11 months to elapse before commencing settlement discussions].) Plaintiff also failed to exercise diligence to schedule or attend Court-ordered arbitration of the matter, causing the Court to issue multiple orders to show cause and the appointed arbitrators to lose jurisdiction. Plaintiff has not carried his burden to excuse his lack of activity or to show excusable delay. (See Lopez v. State of California (1996) 49 Cal.App.4th 1292, 1295; Oskooi v. Fountain Valley Regional Hospital (1996) 42 Cal.App.4th 233, 239-241 [dismissal is mandatory when there is no showing of excusable delay].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JEFFREY KLEIN VS. NEWELL & COMPANY, INC, DBA
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Concannon Construction, Inc.'S Motion For Order Determining Good Faith Settlement.
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, February 5, 2019, 2018, Line 3, Defendant Concannon Construction, Inc.'S Motion For Order Determining Good Faith Settlement. Cross-Defendant Concannon Construction, Inc.'s motion for an order determining good faith settlement is granted. The Court has carefully considered the declarations and documents filed by all three cross-defendants seeking good faith determinations in this case, particularly Plaintiff's interrogatory responses regarding the amount of their claimed damages and the report of Plaintiffs' expert Richard Avelar regarding the causes of the claimed damages and the costs to repair the identified water intrusion and associated damages. The discovery responses, expert report, and other declarations provide sufficient information to support a rough approximation of the Plaintiffs' total recovery and of the moving party's proportionate liability. The Court finds that the amount of the settlement is within the reasonable range of the settling tortfeasor's proportional share of comparative liability for the plaintiff's injuries, and that the other pertinent factors weigh in favor of granting the motion. (See Tech-Bilt, Inc. v. Woodward-Clyde & Assocs. (1985) 38 Cal.3d 488, 499.) Based on information available at the time of the settlement, it is reasonable to conclude that in light of the moving party's limited role in the construction of Plaintiffs' house and the apparent lack of connection between its work and the causes of the water intrusion and associated damages, the moving party had little, if any, potential liability for Plaintiff's claimed damages. (See Cahill v. San Diego Gas & Electric Co. (2011) 194 Cal.App.4th 939, 962-966 [settlement amount of $25,000 was within ballpark of good faith settlements under Code of Civil Procedure section 877.6, despite plaintiff's multi-million dollar damages claim, when plaintiff had little if any chance of recovery at trial].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: GILL REPROGRAPHICS INC. VS. CLINT WALSTON ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion And Notice Of Motion To Stay Proceedings
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, February 5, 2019, 2018, Line 4, DEFENDANT CLINT WALSTON, SYNERGY REPROGRAPHICS, LLC's Motion To Stay Proceedings. (part 1 of 2) Defendants and Cross-Complainants Clint Walton and Synergy Reprographics, LLC's motion to stay this action pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 410.30(a) in favor of the earlier-filed action pending in Texas state court is denied. A trial court considering a forum non conveniens issue engages in a two-step process. The first step is to determine whether a suitable alternative forum exists. (National Football League v. Fireman's Fund Ins. Co. (2013) 216 Cal.App.4th 902, 917.) Where (as here) there is a suitable alternative forum, the court proceeds to the next step, consideration of the private interests of the parties and the public interest in keeping the case in California. (Id.) The private interest factors are those that make trial and the enforceability of the ensuing judgment expeditious and relatively inexpensive, such as the ease of access to sources of proof, the cost of obtaining attendance of witnesses, and the availability of compulsory process for attendance of unwilling witnesses. (Id.) The public interest factors include avoidance of overburdening California courts, protecting potential jurors who should not be called on to decide cases in which the local community has little concern, and weighing the competing ties of California and the alternate jurisdiction to the litigation. (Id.; see also Leadford v. Leadford (1992) 6 Cal.App.4th 571, 575.) The defendant, as the moving party, bears the burden of proof on a motion based on forum non conveniens. (Id. at 918.) The trial court's weighing and balancing of private and public factors is reviewed pursuant to an abuse of discretion standard. (Id.) Here, Defendants have not met their burden to show that the pertinent factors weigh in favor of staying this action in favor of the Texas action. First, while Mr. Walston apparently is now a Texas resident, he alleges in the cross-complaint that he worked in San Francisco from 2006 to 2017 and that his damages were the result of Cross-Defendants' actions while he was located in San Francisco. Further, Synergy, which joined in the motion, apparently has its principal place of business in San Francisco and regularly conducts business in California. Moreover, the gravamen of the action relates to the termination of Mr. Walston's relationship with Plaintiff Gill Reprographics, Inc. in 2017. Many witnesses to the parties' cross-claims against each other, as well as documents and other evidence, therefore are likely to be located in California and subject to the process of this Court. Second, California has a substantial interest in enforcing its policies against misappropriation of trade secrets, unfair competition, intentional interference with contract, and tortious interference with prospective economic relations allegedly arising out of conduct occurring in California and affecting the operation of a California business and its employees. Third, the Texas action was filed only two months before this action, and Defendants did not seek a stay of this action until they filed the instant motion in January 2019, shortly before the approaching March 22, 2019 hearing on Plaintiffs' motion for summary judgment or summary adjudication and April 22, 2019 trial date. (end of part 1 of 2 ) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: DENTAL FORCE, INC. ET AL VS. BEMDAD BEHNOOD ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, February 5, 2019, 2018, Line 6, DEFENDANT BAMDAD BEHNOOD's MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT.(part 1 of 2) Defendant Bemdad Behnood's special motion to strike the third cause of action of the complaint pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 425.16 is denied. Defendant's motion fails the first prong of the anti-SLAPP analysis because she has not met her threshold burden of show that her alleged denigration of Plaintiffs' dental practice constituted statements "made in a place open to the public or as public forum in connection within an issue of public interest" or "any other conduct in furtherance of the exercise of . . . the constitutional right of free speech in connection with a public issue or an issue of public interest." (Code Civ. Proc. § 425.16(e)(3),(4).) Plaintiffs allege that Defendant, who was formerly employed by Plaintiffs as a dental hygienist, in violation of her contractual obligation to maintain the confidentiality of its proprietary information including patient lists and medical records, improperly contacted Plaintiffs' patients and solicited them to leave Plaintiffs' dental practice and join a competing business. (Compl. 11, 13, 14.) The third cause of action, for intentional interference with prospective economic advantage, alleges that Defendant, by utilizing this proprietary information to solicit Plaintiffs' patients, intentionally interfered with Plaintiffs' prospective economic relationship with their patients, resulting in at least 100 patients switching their patronage. (Id. 42-48.) Plaintiffs allege that when Defendant contacted their patients, she bad-mouthed them by stating that "a big corporation has taken over and they employed bad dentists and that the treatment was terrible and they should go with [Defendant]" to her new employer. (Id. 14.) The alleged communications were not made in the context of any public discussion. In order to satisfy the public issue/public interest requirement of the anti-SLAPP statute, "in cases where the issue is not of interest to the public at large, but rather to a limited, but definable portion of the public (a private group, organization, or community), the constitutionally protected activity must, at a minimum, occur in the context of an ongoing controversy, dispute or discussion." (Du Charme v. Int'l Brotherhood of Electrical Workers (2003) 110 Cal.App.4th 107, 119.) The communications at issue here "were not disseminated in the context of any purported ongoing controversy regarding [Plaintiffs'] business practices, nor were they directed at encouraging others to participate in the discussion. [Citation omitted.] Rather, the information was part of a competitor's pitch to [Plaintiffs' patients], and was motivated solely by the competitor's desire to increase its sales ranks. Because the information was contained in solicitations that were designed solely for the purpose of commercial activity, it is not entitled to protection under the anti-SLAPP statute." (World Financial Group v. HBW Ins. & Financial Services, Inc. (2009) 172 Cal.App.4th 1561, 1572-1573.) Defendant, who did not submit any evidence in support of her motion, has failed to show that her alleged criticism of Plaintiffs' dental practice in communications with its patients arose in the context of any public controversy. (See Donovan v. Dan Murphy Foundation (2012) 204 Cal.App.4th 1500, 1508 [anti-SLAPP motion properly denied where defendant "presented no evidence of widespread public interest"].) (end of part 1 of 1)=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: DENTAL FORCE, INC. ET AL VS. BEMDAD BEHNOOD ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Bamdad Behnood'S Motion To Strike Complaint.(Part 2 Of 2)(For Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, February 5, 2019, 2018, Line 6, Defendant Bamdad Behnood'S Motion To Strike Complaint.(Part 2 Of 2)(For Tentative Ruling Only). Even if Plaintiff could establish that her alleged statements in the course of improperly soliciting Defendants' patients were protected, they were at most merely incidental to the conduct on which the complaint is based. As in World Financial Group v. HBW Ins. & Financial Services, Inc. (2009) 172 Cal.App.4th 1561, Defendant's communications were not "designed to inform the public of an issue of public interest. They were merely solicitations of a competitor's employees and customers undertaken for the sole purpose of furthering a business interest." (Id. at 1572 [affirming denial of anti-SLAPP motion on ground that defendants failed to make a prima facie showing that complaint arose from protected activity, where it primarily alleged that defendants misappropriated plaintiff's trade secrets and utilized confidential information to solicit plaintiff's associates and customers]; see also Renewable Resources Coalition, Inc. v. Pebble Mines Corp. (2013) 218 Cal.App.4th 384, 398 [holding that the anti-SLAPP statute does not apply when a claim is based on the defendant improperly purchasing confidential documents for financial gain]."). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (end of part 2 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: EDUARDO PANIAGUA ET AL VS. MILESTONE FINANCIAL, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Milestone Financial, Llc, Bear Bruin Ventures, Inc., William Stuart, Carolyn Stuart, Zoe Hamilton'S Petition/Application To Compel Arbitration And Stay Civil Action. (Part 2 Of 2)(For Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, February 5, 2019, 2018, Line 7, Defendant Milestone Financial, Llc, Bear Bruin Ventures, Inc., William Stuart, Carolyn Stuart, Zoe Hamilton'S Petition/Application To Compel Arbitration And Stay Civil Action. (Part 2 Of 2)(For Tentative Ruling Only). The cease and desist orders attached to the petition to compel arbitration and the findings made by the Bureau of Real Estate in those orders provide substantial evidence that at or around the time Plaintiffs entered into the original residential mortgage loan agreement, Defendants Milestone and Hamilton were misrepresenting to the public that they were licensed to act as a real estate broker, real estate salesperson, and/or mortgage loan broker, when in fact they lacked the real estate broker licenses, real estate licenses, and mortgage loan originator endorsements required by statute. This evidence is sufficient to support "a preliminary factual determination that misleading circumstances existed and led to the entry into the agreements, supporting a conclusion the agreements are void. These conclusions apply to avoid the arbitration clauses contained within the agreements." (Duffens, 161 Cal.App.4th at 456.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.(end of 2 of 2)=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: EDUARDO PANIAGUA ET AL VS. MILESTONE FINANCIAL, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Petition/Application To Compel Arbitration And Petition/Application To Compel Arbitration And Stay Civil Action; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; Declaration Of William R. Stuart
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, February 5, 2019, 2018, Line 7, DEFENDANT MILESTONE FINANCIAL, LLC, BEAR BRUIN VENTURES, INC., WILLIAM STUART, CAROLYN STUART, ZOE HAMILTON's Petition/Application To Compel Arbitration And Stay Civil Action. (part 1 of 2) Defendants Milestone Financial, LLC, Bear Bruin Ventures, Inc., William R. Stuart, Carolyn Stuart, and Zoe Hamilton's petition to compel arbitration and stay civil action is denied. Plaintiffs allege that they were fraudulently induced to enter into the original loan agreement and later loan modification agreements by Defendants' fraudulent misrepresentations and omissions, including their alleged failures to disclose Milestone's unlicensed status and the existence of cease and desist orders against Defendants by the California Bureau of Real Estate. Plaintiffs also seek rescission of those agreements. An arbitration clause generally will not be enforced if it is found within an agreement that may properly be judicially determined to be void for fraud in the execution or fraud in the inception, such that there was never any existing agreement in the first place. (Duffens v. Valenti (2008) 161 Cal.App.4th 434, 448.) Here, as in Duffens, Plaintiffs "essentially plead that fraud in the inducement occurred, because they knew what they were signing, i.e., their agreements. However, they claim their consent was induced by fraud, so that even though there was mutual assent and a contract, the contract, by reason of the fraud, is voidable." (161 Cal.App.4th at 450.) Under California's Real Estate Law, "[i]t is unlawful for any person to engage in the business, act in the capacity of, advertise or assume to act as a real estate broker or a real estate salesman within this state without first obtaining a real estate license . . . ." (Bus. & Prof. Code § 10130.) In addition, "[n]o individual may engage in business as a mortgage loan originator" without first obtaining and maintaining a real estate license and a license endorsement identifying that individual as a licensed mortgage loan originator. (Id. § 10166.02(b).) "The purpose of the licensing requirement is to protect the public from the perils incident to dealing with incompetent or untrustworthy real estate practitioners." (Schantz v. Ellsworth (1971) 19 Cal.App.3d 289, 292-293.) Indeed, an unlicensed person who acts as a real estate broker is subject to penal consequences. (Id. §§ 10139, 10185.) A person "engaged in the business or acting in the capacity of a real estate broker or a real estate salesman" is barred from bringing or maintaining an action "for the collection of compensation for the performance of any of the acts mentioned in this article without alleging and proving he was a duly licensed real estate broker . . . at the time the alleged cause of action arose." (Id. § 10136.) (end of part 1 of 2) =(302/EPS)
